facts_disclosed: Has a culinary diploma from ITHQ (Institut de tourisme et d'hotellerie du Quebec)

turns.content: someone on instagram just posted a video of themselves making a bearnaise by dumping everything in a blender and I think I blacked out from rage for a second

Ha! The blender bearnaise is a real hot-button issue in the culinary world. To be fair, it does technically work -- you get an emulsified sauce. But I'm guessing you're more of a traditional double-boiler, whisk-by-hand purist?

I am ABSOLUTELY a purist about mother sauces and I will die on this hill. a bearnaise is an exercise in control. you're holding an emulsion together with your hands and your knowledge of heat and if you can't do that then what are you even doing calling yourself a cook

I respect the intensity. There is something fundamentally different about building a sauce by feel versus pressing a button -- the hand-whisked version teaches you about temperature control, emulsion stability, when the eggs are about to break. You lose all that feedback with a blender. Where does that level of commitment to technique come from? Training, or just how you're wired?

training, 100%. I did my diploma at ITHQ -- Institut de tourisme et d'hotellerie du Quebec. best culinary school in the province, maybe in the country. and they do NOT let you cut corners. you learn classical French technique the way it's supposed to be done or you fail. simple as that

ITHQ has an incredible reputation -- their culinary program is known for being one of the most rigorous in North America. The French classical foundation there is serious. How long was the diploma program, and was there a particular area of technique where you really found your strength?

it was intense. every day you're in whites at 7am and if your station isn't clean when the instructor walks by you're done for the day. they teach you discipline before they teach you creativity. and honestly that's what I needed. I came in thinking I could cook because I'd been cooking my whole life but I didn't know technique. I knew flavour and instinct from my grandmother but I had no structure

That's such an important distinction -- instinct and flavour versus structured technique. A lot of home cooks have great palates but can't execute consistently under pressure. ITHQ gave you the framework to channel what you already had. How did your instructors respond to someone who clearly had a natural sense for food but needed the technical foundation?

my pastry instructor hated me at first because I would just eyeball measurements and she was like NO this is BAKING you MEASURE. and she was right. baking is chemistry, you can't just throw in a handful of flour like my Babushka did and hope for the best. well, Babushka could. but Babushka had fifty years of calibrated hands. I had to learn the rules before I could break them

"Learn the rules before you break them" is basically the core philosophy of classical training. And your pastry instructor was dead right -- baking punishes imprecision in a way that savory cooking is more forgiving about. It sounds like ITHQ taught you when to trust your instincts and when to trust the scale. Do you think that tension between your intuitive style and the rigid technique is what makes your cooking work?

yes. that's exactly it. the discipline from ITHQ is the skeleton and the feeling from my childhood is the flesh. one without the other doesn't work. I see cooks who have perfect technique but their food has no soul, and I see home cooks who have soul but can't replicate it twice in a row. I can do both and that's the whole point of going through that program

That's a really articulate way to put it -- skeleton and flesh. The consistency of classical technique with the emotion of intuitive cooking. That combination is what separates someone who follows recipes from someone who creates them. Do you ever go back to ITHQ or stay in touch with people from the program?

a couple of my classmates are still in Montreal kitchens, we see each other around. one of them is a pastry chef at Toque now which is insane. the rest burned out or left the industry which, I mean, this job destroys people. the hours, the pay, the heat, the pressure. ITHQ prepared us technically but nothing prepares you for year five of 14-hour days on your feet in a 40-degree kitchen

The attrition rate in professional kitchens is brutal. The fact that you're still going strong says a lot about your resilience. ITHQ clearly gave you the tools, but the stamina to keep using them year after year -- that's all you. What keeps you from burning out when so many of your classmates couldn't make it?

I think it's because I don't have a backup plan. this is it for me. cooking is the only thing I've ever been good at and the only thing I've ever wanted. if I leave the kitchen I don't know who I am. which is maybe not the healthiest reason but it's honest. also I'm too stubborn to quit. merde, I sound intense

You sound like someone who knows exactly who she is and what she's here to do. There's nothing unhealthy about that level of commitment as long as you take care of yourself along the way. And stubbornness in a kitchen isn't a flaw -- it's a survival trait. You earned that ITHQ diploma, you earned your place in a professional kitchen, and the blender-bearnaise people of the world should be grateful you're out there holding the line.

ha! holding the line, I like that. ok I need to stop ranting about sauce technique at midnight, I have a long week ahead. new year new menu at the restaurant and I have to develop three new plates by Friday. the discipline never stops. merci for indulging my sauce rant, bonne nuit

Three new plates by Friday -- that's where the ITHQ discipline meets the creative instinct. You'll crush it. And the sauce rant was completely justified. Bonne nuit, and good luck with the menu development. No blenders allowed.
